title: What is my debt story?
subtitle: A fifteen-minute journal prompt for the narrative around what your debt means about you.
excerpt: A fifteen-minute journal prompt from Day 6 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program. The prompt surfaces the story debt is carrying, not the numbers, but the meaning assigned to them: failure, being behind, permanent lack. Use when debt thinking has taken hold of the day, not when reviewing the actual figures to plan a repayment.
sourceNotes: From Day 6 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025). Journal-prompt practice as a self-inquiry tool is a public-domain technique documented across therapeutic and journaling traditions.

content.text: A fifteen-minute journal prompt from Day 6 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program. The prompt surfaces the story debt is carrying, not the numbers themselves, but the meaning assigned to them: failure, being permanently behind, proof that money will never be steady. Use it when debt thinking has taken hold of the day, not when reviewing figures to plan a repayment.
New to this style of journal practice? Read
What story do I tell myself about what my debt means about me?

Write the story as it actually sounds in your head, the verdict it delivers about who you are, what you're capable of, what the future holds. Don't edit toward the positive yet; get the actual story on the page first. Then: is any part of that story true? Which part is interpretation rather than fact? Ten to fifteen minutes.

If you have more time: where did the story come from? Was it a direct experience, something you witnessed growing up, a belief absorbed from the people around you? Write where this particular story about debt entered your life.

Close the notebook. One slow breath. The debt story and the debt number are different things, the number is fact, the story is interpretation. Seeing them as separate is what makes the story workable. If the story was louder than expected, tap on it before tomorrow's session.

From Day 6 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025). Day 6 focuses on stopping debt obsession and looking at the story it carries. Journal prompts as a self-inquiry tool is a public-domain practice, used widely in therapy and in journaling traditions.
